title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about four people who share how they try to live more sustainably in their daily lives. For questions 43–52, choose from the people (A–D). The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: Living with Less Impact: Four People on Sustainable Choices

texts.C:
### Zoe, 24, Content Creator
[45]I gave up buying new clothes entirely two years ago and now source everything second-hand – charity shops, vintage markets, clothes-swapping events and online resale platforms.[/45] I started documenting this on social media purely for personal accountability, but the audience it attracted was far larger than I expected. [49]I'm very conscious of not presenting sustainable living as glamorous or effortless, because for many people it requires real time and planning that they simply don't have.[/49] I try to be honest about the limitations and trade-offs, and my followers seem to appreciate that honesty, which I think is what distinguishes useful content from greenwashing.
texts.B:
### Nadia, 33, Architect
As an architect, I try to incorporate sustainable design principles into every project, but I've also made significant changes at home. [43]I retrofitted my Victorian terraced house with external wall insulation, a heat pump and a rainwater harvesting system – not cheap, but the energy bills have dropped dramatically and the house is far more comfortable year-round.[/43] The challenge with sustainable renovation is convincing clients that the upfront cost is worth it when the payoff takes years to show up. [47]Some colleagues find my enthusiasm for all this slightly intense, but I find it almost impossible to separate my professional values from my personal choices – the two feel entirely intertwined to me.[/47] [51]I've started running free workshops for homeowners in my area on low-cost sustainable improvements, partly to demonstrate that you don't have to spend a fortune to make a meaningful difference.[/51]
texts.D:
### Gerald, 61, Retired Civil Servant
[46]Since retiring, I've had more time to focus on reducing the waste my household produces, and I've been amazed at how much of it was simply unnecessary habit.[/46] I compost everything, buy loose vegetables from the local market, repair items rather than replacing them, and have reduced my general household waste by around two-thirds. [50]The change I'm most proud of is giving up the car entirely – I now cycle and use public transport, which has also improved my fitness in ways I hadn't anticipated.[/50] Some friends think it's all rather extreme for one person, but someone has to start.
texts.A:
### Patrick, 50, Farmer
[44]I switched from conventional arable farming to regenerative practices six years ago, which involved rebuilding my soil health from scratch and phasing out all artificial fertilisers and pesticides.[/44] The first couple of harvests were nervously small, but the soil has responded remarkably well. [48]The hardest part has been the scepticism from neighbouring farmers who see my methods as impractical idealism – but when they see my soil test results, attitudes are slowly beginning to shift.[/48] I've introduced hedgerows, wildflower strips and beetle banks across the farm, which has brought back wildlife I haven't seen here in decades. [52]The real satisfaction is knowing that I'm passing on land in better condition than when I inherited it – that matters more to me than any profit margin.[/52]

questions.question: made significant physical changes to their home specifically to reduce energy consumption?
questions.explanation: Nadia retrofitted her house with insulation, a heat pump and a rainwater harvesting system, resulting in dramatically lower energy bills.

questions.question: abandoned a traditional professional method in order to adopt more environmentally responsible practices?
questions.explanation: Patrick switched from conventional arable farming to regenerative practices, phasing out all artificial fertilisers and pesticides.

questions.question: has stopped acquiring new products of a certain kind and obtains them through alternative means instead?
questions.explanation: Zoe stopped buying new clothes and now sources everything second-hand through charity shops, markets and resale platforms.

questions.question: found that having more free time allowed them to address habits that had previously been wasteful?
questions.explanation: Gerald says that since retiring he has had more time to focus on reducing waste and was amazed at how much was simply unnecessary habit.

questions.question: notes that people in their professional environment find their personal commitment somewhat excessive?
questions.explanation: Nadia says some colleagues find her enthusiasm slightly intense and that she cannot separate her professional values from her personal choices.

questions.question: describes facing doubt or resistance from others working in the same field?
questions.explanation: Patrick says neighbouring farmers see his regenerative methods as impractical idealism, though attitudes are beginning to shift.

questions.question: is careful to present a realistic rather than an idealistic picture of sustainable living?
questions.explanation: Zoe says she is conscious of not presenting sustainable living as glamorous or effortless, because it requires real time and planning.

questions.question: describes giving up a significant convenience in exchange for both environmental and health benefits?
questions.explanation: Gerald gave up his car entirely and now cycles and uses public transport, which has also improved his fitness.

questions.question: describes using their professional expertise to help others make sustainable changes at little or no cost?
questions.explanation: Nadia runs free workshops for homeowners on low-cost sustainable improvements to demonstrate that big spending is not required.

questions.question: says that improving conditions for future generations matters more to them than immediate personal or financial reward?
questions.explanation: Patrick says the real satisfaction is knowing he is passing on land in better condition than when he inherited it, which matters more than profit.
